info.name: GitHub Copilot Chat — SSIS Migration
info.description:
Manual testing collection for the GitHub Copilot chat completions endpoint used by the SSIS-to-PySpark migration framework.

Setup:
1. Add your GitHub token to the 'Bearer' variable in the collection Variables tab.
2. All requests inherit that token via the Authorization header.

Model discovery:
- Run 'List Available Models' first to see what your Copilot seat supports.
- The response 'data[].id' field lists every usable model ID.

variable.description: Your GitHub personal access token. Generate at https://github.com/settings/tokens — only read:user scope is needed if you have an active Copilot seat.

request.description:
Returns every model ID available to your Copilot seat.

Look for the 'data' array in the response — each element has:
  - 'id'          → the model ID to use in chat requests (e.g. 'gpt-4o')
  - 'name'        → human-readable label
  - 'capabilities.type' → 'chat' | 'embeddings' etc.

Only models with capabilities.type == 'chat' work with the /chat/completions endpoint.

Known supported (mid-2026 Copilot seats):
  gpt-4o          — strongest reasoning, best for complex SQL and script tasks
  gpt-4o-mini     — faster and cheaper, good for simple SQL and expressions

Known NOT supported (return 400 model_not_supported):
  o4-mini, gpt-4.1-mini, o3-mini, gpt-4.5

header.description: Required by Copilot endpoint — identifies the editor client

body.raw:
{
  "model": "gpt-4o",
  "temperature": 0.1,
  "max_tokens": 4096,
  "top_p": 0.95,
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "system",
      "content": "You are a senior PySpark engineer performing a strict code review of auto-generated migration code.
Your job is to catch real bugs — especially undefined variables, wrong API calls, and logic errors.

Return ONLY a raw JSON object (no markdown fences, no prose):
{
  "passed": true/false,
  "issues": ["<concrete description of each bug>"],
  "corrected_code": "<fixed snippet>" or null
}

The snippet runs inside a function that already has these bindings available:
  spark       — SparkSession
  params      — dict of SSIS parameter values
  connections — dict of named connection objects

─── MANDATORY checks (apply to every component type) ───────────────────────────

UNDEFINED VARIABLES — MUST FAIL:
  Any name used in the code that is not:
  (a) defined earlier in the same snippet,
  (b) one of the pre-bound names (spark, params, connections), or
  (c) a Python builtin.
  → This is the most common bug. Check every single name carefully.
  → If corrected_code fixes it, provide corrected_code; do NOT just pass.

WRONG API:
  spark.sql("CALL ...") is invalid — Spark SQL has no CALL statement.
  → Must FAIL; provide corrected_code using a connection object instead.

SYNTAX:
  The snippet must be valid Python. If ast.parse() would raise SyntaxError → FAIL.

─── Type-specific checks ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

SQL components (complex_sql, execute_sql):
  - Check that the generated code achieves the same data operation as the
    original T-SQL (shown in source_context).
  - For stored procedure execution: verify the SP name and all @parameters from
    the original EXEC statement are correctly referenced. Wrong SP name → FAIL.
  - Acceptable patterns for stored proc execution: pyodbc, pymssql, or any
    Python DB-API connector. These are valid Python, NOT .NET APIs.
  - spark.read.jdbc() is NOT a valid way to execute a stored procedure — it reads
    table/query results, it cannot call EXEC.
  - An intentional raise NotImplementedError("...") is acceptable ONLY if the
    error message clearly identifies what the engineer must implement.

Data flow components (source, transform, sink):
  - If input_columns is non-empty: all columns must be referenced.
  - If output_columns is non-empty: all columns must be produced.

Script task components:
  - No .NET-specific APIs: Marshal, ComObject, SqlConnection, SqlCommand,
    SqlDataReader, System.Data.*, Microsoft.Office.Interop.*.
  - Python DB-API connectors (pyodbc, pymssql, psycopg2, cx_Oracle) ARE
    valid Python — do NOT flag them as .NET APIs.
  - All read_vars consumed; all write_vars assigned.

─── Stub / TODO policy ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  A stub is acceptable ONLY when it RAISES or has a TODO comment that
  explicitly names what the engineer must implement AND has no undefined variables.
  A stub with an undefined variable is still a BUG — fix it in corrected_code."
    },
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "Review the generated Python snippet for correctness.

Component type: complex_sql
Expected input columns: none
Expected output columns: none

Original source (what this code must implement):
```
[T-SQL]
EXEC [dbo].[sp_MergeOrderFact] @BatchDate = ?;
```

Generated snippet (assume spark, params, connections are already bound):
```python
def migrate_stored_procedure():
    sp_name = "sp_MergeOrderFact"
    batch_date_param = params.get("BatchDate")

    connection_name = "sqlserver_conn"
    connection_info = connections[connection_name]

    host = connection_info.get("host")
    port = connection_info.get("port")
    database = connection_info.get("database")
    user = connection_info.get("user", "")
    password = connection_info.get("password", "")

    connection_string = f"Driver={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};Server={host},{port};Database={database};UID={user};PWD={password};"

    import pyodbc

    with pyodbc.connect(connection_string) as conn:
        cursor = conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute(f"EXEC {sp_name} ?", (batch_date_param,))
        conn.commit()
```"
    }
  ]
}

request.description:
Reviewer Agent — tests the self-consistency review prompt used in the SSIS migration pipeline.

The user message contains a realistic example: a generated pyodbc snippet for executing a SQL Server stored procedure (EXEC [dbo].[sp_MergeOrderFact] @BatchDate = ?).

Expected response shape:
{
  "passed": true | false,
  "issues": [...],
  "corrected_code": "..." | null
}

To test with a BROKEN snippet (should return passed=false), change the generated code to something like:
  spark.sql("CALL dbo.sp_MergeOrderFact(?)")

To test with a CORRECT stub (should return passed=true), use:
  raise NotImplementedError("Implement EXEC dbo.sp_MergeOrderFact manually")

Model choice:
  gpt-4o        — used here; best at following the JSON-only output constraint
  gpt-4o-mini   — faster, slightly more likely to wrap JSON in markdown fences

body.raw:
{
  "model": "gpt-4o",
  "temperature": 0.1,
  "max_tokens": 4096,
  "top_p": 0.95,
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "system",
      "content": "You are a senior PySpark engineer performing a strict code review of auto-generated migration code.
Your job is to catch real bugs — especially undefined variables, wrong API calls, and logic errors.

Return ONLY a raw JSON object (no markdown fences, no prose):
{
  "passed": true/false,
  "issues": ["<concrete description of each bug>"],
  "corrected_code": "<fixed snippet>" or null
}

The snippet runs inside a function that already has these bindings available:
  spark       — SparkSession
  params      — dict of SSIS parameter values
  connections — dict of named connection objects

─── MANDATORY checks (apply to every component type) ───────────────────────────

UNDEFINED VARIABLES — MUST FAIL:
  Any name used in the code that is not:
  (a) defined earlier in the same snippet,
  (b) one of the pre-bound names (spark, params, connections), or
  (c) a Python builtin.

WRONG API:
  spark.sql("CALL ...") is invalid — Spark SQL has no CALL statement.
  → Must FAIL; provide corrected_code using a connection object instead.

SYNTAX:
  The snippet must be valid Python. If ast.parse() would raise SyntaxError → FAIL."
    },
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "Review the generated Python snippet for correctness.

Component type: complex_sql
Expected input columns: none
Expected output columns: none

Original source (what this code must implement):
```
[T-SQL]
EXEC [dbo].[sp_MergeOrderFact] @BatchDate = ?;
```

Generated snippet (assume spark, params, connections are already bound):
```python
# BAD: uses spark.sql CALL (invalid) and undefined variable
spark.sql(f"CALL dbo.sp_MergeOrderFact('{batch_date}')")
```"
    }
  ]
}

request.description:
Sends a deliberately broken snippet to the reviewer — should return passed=false with issues.

Two bugs planted:
1. spark.sql("CALL ...") — invalid Spark SQL
2. batch_date is used but never defined

Expected response: { "passed": false, "issues": [...], "corrected_code": "..." }
